title: The Money Printer and the Emperor
synopsis: The Federal Reserve has printed approximately three trillion dollars since March. The Reserve Bank of Australia has launched its first-ever quantit...
preview: The Federal Reserve has printed approximately three trillion dollars since March. The Reserve Bank of Australia has launched its first-ever quantit...
content:
The Federal Reserve has printed approximately three trillion dollars since March. The Reserve Bank of Australia has launched its first-ever quantitative easing program. The European Central Bank has expanded its bond-buying by an additional seven hundred and fifty billion euros. The Bank of Japan, which had already been printing money for decades, has accelerated.

The numbers are too large to comprehend, which is, I suspect, part of why they generate less alarm than they should. A trillion dollars is a number that has no referent in human experience. You cannot visualise it. You cannot feel it. It is an abstraction piled on an abstraction, and the mind slides off it like water off glass.

So let me make it concrete.

The United States government is spending money it does not have, in quantities it has never spent before, and funding the spending by creating new money. I am describing the mechanism, not characterising it. The Treasury issues bonds. The Federal Reserve buys them with dollars that did not previously exist. The dollars enter the economy. The debt accumulates on the national balance sheet.

In nine years, I wrote an essay about the patterns I was observing in America, and one of the patterns I identified was the parallel between late Roman currency debasement and modern monetary policy. The comparison was speculative then. It feels considerably less speculative now.

The Roman Empire, in its declining centuries, repeatedly debased its currency to fund expenditures that its tax base could not support. The denarius, which had been nearly pure silver under Augustus, contained barely five percent silver by the reign of Gallienus. This was not done out of malice or stupidity. It was done because the alternative, genuine reform of the underlying economic structure, was politically impossible. The legions needed paying. The people needed bread. The borders needed defending. The money was not there, so the money was made less valuable, and the difference between what the money was worth and what the government pretended it was worth became the hidden tax that funded the empire's continuation.

Watch what is happening now.

The United States cannot fund its current commitments from tax revenue. This was true before the pandemic and has become dramatically more true since. The deficit has exploded. The spending is enormous, and much of it is necessary, pandemic relief, unemployment support, the basic maintenance of a society in crisis. The spending may well have been necessary. The mechanism for funding it has historical parallels that should concern anyone who takes those parallels seriously.

When a state cannot address structural problems directly, it reaches for monetary manipulation as a substitute for reform. The Roman emperor debased the coin. The modern central bank prints money. The mechanism is different. The pattern is the same. The can gets kicked down the road, and the road shortens with each kick.

The consequences of money printing are not evenly distributed. This is the part that receives insufficient attention, perhaps because the people who discuss monetary policy professionally are disproportionately among the beneficiaries.

When new money enters the economy, it flows first to financial assets. Stocks rise. Property values increase. Bond prices elevate. The people who own these assets become wealthier, not because they have created anything or produced anything, but because the denominator has changed. Their assets are priced in dollars, and there are more dollars, so the price goes up.

People who do not own financial assets, who sell their labour for wages, who rent rather than own, who save in cash rather than invest, experience the opposite. Their wages do not increase at the rate of asset price inflation. Their rents rise as property values rise. Their savings, held in a currency that is being diluted, lose purchasing power.

The money printer is a regressive tax that nobody voted for. It transfers wealth from the young to the old, from the assetless to the asset-rich, from the bottom of the distribution to the top. It does this silently, through a mechanism that most people do not understand and that is never presented for democratic approval.

In Australia, the Reserve Bank has joined the printing. The amounts are smaller, we are a smaller economy, but the principle is the same. Interest rates at historic lows. Bond purchases that would have been unthinkable a year ago. The explicit intention of pushing asset prices higher, presented as economic stimulus but functioning, in practice, as a further subsidy to asset ownership.

House prices in Australia were already disconnected from wages before the pandemic. They will become more disconnected. The money being printed will find its way into property, because property is where Australian money has always found its way, because the tax system and the cultural orientation and the regulatory settings all point in the same direction. Young Australians who could not afford a house in 2019 will be further from affording one in 2021.

I do not know how this ends. The Roman pattern suggests it ends badly, but slowly, so slowly that each generation can tell itself that things are roughly normal, that the debasement is manageable, that the system will self-correct. It did not self-correct in Rome. The debasement continued until the currency ceased to function as a reliable store of value, and the economy reorganised around different foundations.

Whether the modern version follows the same trajectory is genuinely uncertain. Modern economies are vastly more complex than ancient ones. Central banks have tools that Roman emperors did not. The capacity for correction exists, at least in theory.

But theory requires political will, and political will requires a public that understands what is happening, and the mechanism is specifically designed to be difficult to understand. Quantitative easing. Asset purchase programs. Yield curve control. The language is technical, deliberately so. The emperor's debasement was at least visible, you could weigh the coin and see that it was lighter. The modern debasement is invisible, hidden in policy announcements that most people do not read and would not understand if they did.

The money printer goes brrr, as the internet meme has it. The joke is funny because it is accurate. The question is who pays, and the answer is: the people who are least able to understand the mechanism by which they are paying. The people at the bottom. The young. The assetless. The ones who sell labour rather than own capital.

The emperor debased the currency because reform was politically impossible. The Fed prints because reform is politically impossible. The pattern rhymes. Whether it resolves the same way remains to be seen. But I would not bet against the pattern.
